---
title: "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity"
shorttitle: "Bayesian models of thermal tolerance"
authors:
  - name: "Daniel W.A. Noble"
    email: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Pieter A. Arnold"
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Patrice Pottier"
    email: patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
    affiliations:
      - ref: gu
      - ref: unsw
affiliations:
  - id: anu
    name: Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University
    city: Canberra
    country: Australia
  - id: gu
    name: Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg
    city: Gothenburg
    country: Sweden
  - id: unsw
    name: Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales
    city: Sydney
    state: New South Wales
    country: Australia
abstract: |
  The thermal load sensitivity (TLS) and thermal death time (TDT) frameworks are gold standard approaches for estimating thermal tolerance and predict organismal vulnerability under natural thermal regimes. However, these frameworks are usually implemented through a two-step pipeline. Median times to lethal or sublethal endpoints ($t_{50}$) are first estimated independently across a range of assay temperatures, and these $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are then regressed against temperature. This two-step approach violates key statistical assumptions that are likely to bias the mean and variance in derived quantities such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), critical thermal limits ($CT_{max}$), critical temperatures ($T_{crit}$), heat-injury predictions, and survival probabilities.

  We present a flexible Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model that estimates survival or sublethal responses as a joint function of temperature and exposure duration. We demonstrate how such an approach recovers all the classical outputs of the two-stage modelling framework (i.e., $z$, $CT_{max}$, $T_{crit}$, heat-injury accumulation, survival probabilities), while propagating uncertainty through all derived quantities.  Using simulated data and empirical case studies in brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), zebrafish (*Danio rerio*), and snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*), we demonstrate the benefits of this new modelling framework. 

  We show that the classical and Bayesian frameworks are equivalent, but that the Bayesian approach delivers properly propagated uncertainty, can account for complex hierarchical data (i.e., random effects), can accommodate broader data inputs and overdispersion, and unlocks outputs the two-step pipeline cannot provide, including survival landscape surfaces, between-group contrasts, and inference about the variation in the shape of thermal dose-response curves among groups. We provide a new *R* package ($bayesTLS$) and recommend the Bayesian four-parameter logistic model as the default approach for analysing TDT and TLS data, particularly when the goal is to make accurate predictions of survival under natural thermal regimes.
keywords:
  - thermal death time
  - thermal tolerance landscape
  - hierarchical Bayesian model
  - 4-parameter logistic
  - heat injury
  - critical thermal maximum
  - ectotherm
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  docx:
    reference-doc: ../bib/templates/template.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: "H"
    colorlinks: true
    link-citations: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % tinytable LaTeX dependencies. See supplement.qmd for rationale.
        \usepackage{tabularray}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayUnderline}[1]{\underline{#1}}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayStrikeout}[1]{\sout{#1}}
        % Author block: Quarto's default \author{names \and …} gives
        % names only. We override with authblk to pin affiliations from
        % YAML to the title page.
        \usepackage[affil-it]{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authsep{, }
        \renewcommand\Authand{, }
        \renewcommand\Authands{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\itshape\small}
        \let\OldAuthor\author
        \renewcommand{\author}[1]{}
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Daniel W.A. Noble\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{daniel.noble@anu.edu.au}}}%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Pieter A. Arnold}%
          \OldAuthor[2,3]{Patrice Pottier}%
          \affil[1]{Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia}%
          \affil[2]{Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden}%
          \affil[3]{Evolution \& Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia}%
        }
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Daniel W.A. Noble**^1,\*^, **Pieter A. Arnold**^1^, **Patrice Pottier**^2,3^

^1^Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia.
^2^Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden.
^3^Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.
^\*^Corresponding author: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au

**Abstract**

The thermal load sensitivity (TLS) and thermal death time (TDT) frameworks are gold standard approaches for estimating thermal tolerance and predict organismal vulnerability under natural thermal regimes. However, these frameworks are usually implemented through a two-step pipeline. Median times to lethal or sublethal endpoints ($t_{50}$) are first estimated independently across a range of assay temperatures, and these $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are then regressed against temperature. This two-step approach violates key statistical assumptions that are likely to bias the mean and variance in derived quantities such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), critical thermal limits ($CT_{max}$), critical temperatures ($T_{crit}$), heat-injury predictions, and survival probabilities.

We present a flexible Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model that estimates survival or sublethal responses as a joint function of temperature and exposure duration. We demonstrate how such an approach recovers all the classical outputs of the two-stage modelling framework (i.e., $z$, $CT_{max}$, $T_{crit}$, heat-injury accumulation, survival probabilities), while propagating uncertainty through all derived quantities.  Using simulated data and empirical case studies in brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), zebrafish (*Danio rerio*), and snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*), we demonstrate the benefits of this new modelling framework. 

We show that the classical and Bayesian frameworks are equivalent, but that the Bayesian approach delivers properly propagated uncertainty, can account for complex hierarchical data (i.e., random effects), can accommodate broader data inputs and overdispersion, and unlocks outputs the two-step pipeline cannot provide, including survival landscape surfaces, between-group contrasts, and inference about the variation in the shape of thermal dose-response curves among groups. We provide a new *R* package ($bayesTLS$) and recommend the Bayesian four-parameter logistic model as the default approach for analysing TDT and TLS data, particularly when the goal is to make accurate predictions of survival under natural thermal regimes.

**Keywords:** thermal-death-time; thermal tolerance landscape; multilevel model; 4-parameter logistic; heat injury; heat tolerance; critical thermal maximum; ectotherm
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| cache: false

# Project-wide objects loaded for inline reporting throughout the manuscript.
# Every number, table, and figure is produced from code — no magic numbers.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(brms)
library(posterior)
library(tinytable)

# Force tinytable to register its LaTeX preamble dependencies on every
# render. See supplement.qmd setup-supp for explanation.
invisible(tinytable::tt(data.frame(x = 1)))

set.seed(123)

# Shared figure theme: keep all manuscript figures visually consistent.
# Define once here; load via source() into every figure chunk.
# source(here::here("R", "theme_ms.R"))

# Cached fits (produced by R/bayesian_TDT_shrimp.qmd or equivalent scripts).
# Fits are saved as .rds in output/models/ — never re-fit at render time.
# fit_4pl <- readRDS(here::here("output", "models", "fit_lethal_TDT_4pl.rds"))
# fit_sub <- readRDS(here::here("output", "models", "fit_sublethal_TDT.rds"))
# fit_ctmax <- readRDS(here::here("output", "models", "fit_ctmax.rds"))
```

# Introduction

Climate change is increasing the frequency, intensity, and duration of extreme heat events globally, with cascading consequences for organismal fitness, population persistence, and biodiversity[@snook_fertility_2026; @arnold_thermal_2025; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @smale_marine_2019; @wiens_localextinctions_2016]. Quantifying variation in thermal tolerance within and among species is therefore central to forecasting biological responses to climate change in nature [@sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019; @jorgensen_nature_2022]. Threshold-based measures of heat tolerance, such as the critical thermal maximum ($CT_{max}$), have long been used to establish differences in climate vulnerability among taxa and predicting vulnerability to climate change [@pottier_amphibians_2025; @sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019]. While useful, threshold-based metrics do not capture how the biological effects of extreme heat on fitness depend on both the duration and intensity of heat stress, limiting our ability to make realistic predictions of the impacts of climate change in nature [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021].

The Thermal Death Time (TDT) / Thermal Load Sensitivity (TLS) models address this limitation by quantifying how the intensity and duration of heat exposure jointly determine survival responses to heat stress [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @arnold_thermal_2025]. Recently, the TDT framework was expanded to include sublethal (e.g., fertility, heat coma) responses to heat stress, providing a flexible framework to broadly explain variation in biological responses to heat stress [@orsted_suzukii_2024; @arnold_thermal_2025; @noble2026]. These models capture how heat injury accumulates in a dose-dependent manner above a certain threshold ($T_{crit}$), with injury accumulating more rapidly at higher temperatures and over longer exposures. As a result, organisms may eventually reach a thermal load that leads to compromised physiological function that negatively impacts growth, reproduction and survival [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_landscapes_2014; @arnold_thermal_2025; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019] if injury cannot be repaired [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @jorgensen_unifying_2021]. By linking biological responses to the intensity and duration of heat stress, the TLS and TDT frameworks allow for more realistic predictions of the impacts of  fluctuating thermal regimes  [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021], and have shown promise in predicting observed mortality rates in nature [@rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @zamora2026].

Despite their predictive power, TDT/TLS analyses traditionally involves a two-step modelling approach [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023]. First, organisms are exposed to multiple temperatures for different durations, and the proportion of individuals that are dead, infertile, knocked down, or otherwise affected is modelled as a function of exposure duration. This is typically done by fitting independent dose-response curves (usually models with strong assumptions) at each assay temperature. From these curves, the exposure duration required to reach a specified response threshold is estimated, most often $t_{50}$, the time required to reach 50% mortality, infertility, knockdown, or  other endpoints. Second, the resulting $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are regressed against assay temperature. Thermal sensitivity (i.e., the inverse of the slope of this regression, $z$) and static critical thermal maximum at time $t$ (i.e., the intercept of the regression, $sCT_{max_{t}}$) are then derived from this linear model [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022]. Thermal sensitivity ($z$) is interpreted as the temperature increase required to reduce $t_{50}$ by a factor of 10, while $sCT_{max_{t}}$ is usually arbitrarily computed for a 1 min [@rezende_landscapes_2014], 1 hour [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019], or 4 hour [@orsted_suzukii_2024] duration of heat stress exposure. These quantities can then be used to estimate cumulative Heat Injury (HI) under realistic temperature-time series, where injury accumulates as a function of the exposure duration relative to the predicted tolerance time at each temperature [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @villeneuve_dynamic_2024]. Recent extensions have emphasized that injury accumulation may also be partly offset by repair or acclimation mechanisms, motivating the emergence of new models that incorporate damage-repair dynamics [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @orsted2026].

The two-step pipeline is intuitive and easy to implement, yet it has several practical and statistical limitations [for a recent discission of some issues see @bullard2026]. First, the two-step pipeline does not typically allow proper uncertainty propagation. The $t_{50}$ estimates derived in the first step have non-negligible sampling variance, but they are commonly treated as fixed point estimates in the second-step regression. Neglecting sampling variance can bias or miscalibrate inference on $z$ and $sCT_{max}$, and lead to inflated $R^2$ values. Second, estimating $t_{50}$ from dose-response models requires empirical data spanning a large mortality range (preferably ranging from 0 to 100% mortality) at every assayed temperature to anchor the upper and lower asymptotes of the dose-response curve. When data are sparse or do not span the full mortality range, $t_{50}$ estimates can be incorrectly estimated or undefined, which is then propagated to bias and miscalibrate downstream estimates of $z$ and $CT_{max}$. Third, group comparisons (between species, sexes, or life stages) inherit this miscalibrated uncertainty. Reducing each dose-response curve to a fixed $t_{50}$ estimate makes it difficult to determine if differences in thermal tolerance and sensitivity between groups can be attributed to differences in the upper asymptote, lower asymptote, or curve steepness of the dose-response curve. Curve steepness has recently been shown to affect predictions [@bullard2026]. Fourth, fitting separate dose-response models for each assay temperature and subsequently regressing $t_{50}$ estimates against temperature assumes that these estimates are independent, and typically ignores the hierarchical structure of experimental data (e.g., individuals nested within vials, plates, tanks, clutches, batches, populations, or experimental days). Ignoring this nesting structure prevents the decomposition of variance, and can lead to pseudoreplication and anticonservative uncertainty estimates, particularly when replicate-level variation affects the shape or position of the dose-response curve [@bullard2026]. Finally, the two-step pipeline does not allow downstream predictions of heat injury and survival under fluctuating thermal regimes with uncertainty, which is key to inferring the confidence in our predictions of the impacts of extreme heat impacts. 

Here, we present a single-stage alternative to the classical two-step pipeline: a hierarchical Bayesian four-parameter logistic model fitted jointly to all temperature-by-duration response data. In this framework, survival or sublethal response probabilities are estimated directly as a function of exposure duration and assay temperature, while allowing the parameters of the dose-response curve to vary among groups and hierarchical levels. The four-parameter logistic model provides a flexible sigmoidal description of thermal dose responses, with interpretable parameters for the lower asymptote, upper asymptote, curve steepness, and midpoint [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_ecotox_2010; @nielsen_nlmm_2004; @rudemo_random_1989; @ritz_streibig_2008]. We focus on binomial and beta-binomial outcomes because count data dominate TDT/TLS studies, but the same modelling logic can be extended to other response types using appropriate likelihoods, including continuous performance traits, or latency data. Similarly, although we use the four-parameter log-logistic form for its flexibility, simpler nested models (3PL, with one asymptote fixed; 2PL, with both fixed at 0 and 1) or alternative monotonic dose-response functions (e.g., Weibull, log-normal) can be implemented within the same general framework [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_streibig_2008], however, we show that assumptions of these simplier models can affect TDT/TLS parameter estimations.

We show how classical TDT/TLS quantities, such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), critical temperature tresholds ($T_{crit}$), heat injury accumulation, survival under fluctuating thermal regimes, and their uncertainty, can be derived from this single Bayesian model. We develop these tools into an R package $bayesTLS$, and recommend this new modelling approach as the default approach for analysing organismal responses to varying thermal loads. Using simulations and empirical case studies in brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), zebrafish (*Danio rerio*), Drosophila and plant Acacia photosystem II function, we demonstrate how to implement, validate, and expand this new modelling approach.

```{r}
#| label: fig-joint-4pl-conceptual
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Conceptual comparison of the classical two-stage TDT/TLS workflow and the joint Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model. (a) In the two-stage workflow, separate dose-response curves are fit at each assay temperature and the resulting $t_{50}$ estimates are regressed against temperature. This can fail when assays do not bracket $t_{50}$, treats first-stage estimates as fixed and independent, and does not naturally propagate uncertainty, covariates, or hierarchical structure into $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$. (b) The joint 4PL model estimates the full survival surface in one model. Coloured curves show posterior median survival probabilities at assay temperatures, with shaded pointwise 95% credible intervals. Filled circles mark the model midpoint, `mid(T)`, defining relative $\\log_{10}t_{50}$; open triangles mark the absolute 50% survival crossing. The black and steelblue trajectories give the relative and absolute $t_{50}$ relationships used to derive $z_{rel}$, $z_{abs}$, and $CT_{max,1h}$ at the 60-min reference. Dashed ghost curves show the fitted dose response at the corresponding relative and absolute $CT_{max,1h}$ values. Inset: the 4PL parameters `low`, `up`, `mid`, and `k`. Values are illustrative."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig1.png"))
```

# A non-linear Bayesian hierarchical model for characterizing thermal load sensitivity

## Model specification

Assuming we have mortality data for different times and temperatures, let $y$ denote the number of survivors out of $n$ individuals exposed for time $t$ (e.g., minutes) at assay temperature $T$ (°C). We can model the counts directly with a binomial likelihood (but a beta-binomial likelihood function can also be used and controls for overdispersion):

$$
y \mid n, p \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n,\, p),
$$ {#eq-likelihood}

with survival probability $p$ given by a four-parameter logistic (4PL) function of $\log_{10}$-exposure time,

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10} t - \text{mid})\bigr)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl}

where $\text{low}$ is the lower survival probability asymptote, $\text{up}$ is the upper survival probability asymptote, and $k$ is the slope defining the change in survival probability on the $\log_{10} t$ axis. Larger $k$ gives a sharper decline in survival probability with $\log_{10} t$. $\text{mid}$ is the value of $\log_{10} t$ at which $p$ falls to the midpoint between $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$. When asymptotes are near 0 and 1, $\text{mid}$ coincides with $\log_{10}t_{50}$. While other logistic forms can be used (e.g., two or 3 parameter logistics), @eq-4pl is the most flexible model that can account for different asymptotic survival probabilities.

The key parameters of @eq-4pl (i.e., $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, $k$, $\text{mid}$), can be fit as a non-linear function within a likelihood or Bayesian framework, which estimates their correlation and allows for fixed or random effects to be estimated to explain variation in these parameters. Such an approach allows for *a priori* comparisons (e.g., do sexes or species differ in midpoints?), with correct inference (e.g., do species differ significantly from each other?) and allows for variance decomposition to help identify key sources of variance in the data (e.g., estimating variances explained across populations, species, trials etc.). Such models are most flexibly fit within a Bayesian framework with intuitive interpretation of these parameters and a highly flexible path for error propagation. We show below how such a model can be used to derive all thermal load sensitivity parameters of interest to ecophysiologists. 

# Deriving key thermal load sensitivity parameters 

The TDT/TLS frameworks are elegant in that they derive metrics with clear biological interpretation that can be compared across populations and species (e.g., *z*, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$). Here, we show that we can derive these same metrics directly from @eq-4pl. 

As indicated above, we can model changes in the mean of any of the four parameters in @eq-4pl. Thermal sensitivity (*z*) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ can be derived by modelling the effect of temperature on midpoints specifically as follows:

$$
\text{mid} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar{T}),
$$ {#eq-mid-temp}

where $\text{mid}$ is the $\log_{10}$-time at which survival equals the midpoint between the lower and upper asymptotes at assay temperature $T$ (°C); $\beta_0$ is the intercept giving the midpoint at the grand mean temperature $\bar{T}$; $\beta_1$ is the slope describing how the midpoint changes per °C increase in $T$; and $\bar{T}$ is the grand mean of assay temperatures used to mean-centre $T$, which decorrelates $\beta_0$ from $\beta_1$ and improves parameter estimation and interpretation [@schielzeth_centering_2010].

When the experimental design estimates upper and lower limits of the response (i.e., $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$) near 0 and 1, the midpoint coincides with the classical $t_{50}$ (time to reach 50% survival). Therefore, the regression in @eq-mid-temp directly provides thermal sensitivity as $z = -1/\beta_1$, and the critical thermal limit at any reference time $t_\text{ref}$ is $CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) = \bar{T} + (\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0)/\beta_1$ (with $t_\text{ref} = 60$ min for $CT_{max_{1hr}}$). 

The slope $\beta_1$ — and hence $z$ — is robust to changes in asymptotes. However, threshold summaries, such as $CT_{max}$ and $\log_{10}t_{50}$, are not. When partial mortality or sparse data do not estimate the asymptotes to be around 0 and 1, an asymmetry correction must be added to estimate these threshold summaries when using survival thresholds. For $\log_{10}t_{50}$ this involves adding the following correction factor (@eq-t50) (See Supplement Section 9 for the derivation): 

$$
\log_{10} t_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-t50}

For $CT_{max}$, the correction factor must be subtracted, after division by the slope (to convert the vertical shift in $\log_{10}t_{50}$ into the corresponding shift in temperature), from the naive $CT_{max}$ (@eq-ctmax-corrected):

$$
CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) \;=\; \bar{T} + \frac{\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0}{\beta_1} \;-\; \frac{1}{\beta_1}\cdot\tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-ctmax-corrected}

While we have focused on $t_{50}$ it is important to recognise that any specific survival probability could be used oustide the 50% probability threshold. For example, $t_{10}$ marks the onset of early population mortality – useful for risk management - whereas $t_{90}$ is closer to population-collapse. Luckily, any survival threshold can be modelled using the same posterior by adjusting the threshold in an asymmetry correction that is added to $\text{mid}$ (@eq-LTx): 

$$
\log_{10} t_x(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,\log\!\frac{\text{up} - x/100}{x/100 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-LTx}

$CT_{max}$ is then adjusted in accordance through the correction factor in @eq-ctmax-corrected. Importantly, there may be situations where computing absolute $\log_{10} t_{x}$ based on a sample reaching a given probability threshold is less ideal because of differences in baseline survival at the start of experiments. Instead, one might be interested in calculating what we refer to as 'relative $t_{50}$', which we define as the time at which survival reaches the midpoint between the upper and lower asymptotes (i.e., $p = (\text{up}+\text{low})/2$). By construction, $\text{mid}$ *is* relative $t_{50}$ at every temperature, so no asymmetry correction is needed. $CT_{max}$ is read directly from @eq-mid-temp as $CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) = \bar{T} + (\log_{10}t_\text{ref}-\beta_0)/\beta_1$. Note that relative and absolute $CT_{max}$ are numerically equal only when $\text{up}=1$ and $\text{low}=0$.

A powerful aspect of the Bayesian modelling approach described above is that uncertainty in all of the estimated quantities can be derived. Additinally, each of parameters in the model (an therefore *z* and $CT_{max}$) can be estimated separately for different strata (e.g., sex, population, species) in a single joint model by including the relevant fixed covariates and/or random effects in the linear predictor for $\text{mid}$ — and, if appropriate, in the linear predictors for $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, and $k$ (see @eq-linpred below).

# Predicting heat injury and survival under dynamic thermal environments

A particularly powerful aspect of TDT/TLS models are their ability to translate dynamic temperature times series' from nature to predict heat injury (HI) accumulation and thus population survival probability [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @li2023; @orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. Heat injury accumulates as the time spent at a given temperature divided by the median lethal time at that temperature, integrated across the temperature trajectory $T(i)$ as follows:

$$
HI(t) \;=\; 100 \int_0^t 10^{\,\big(T(i)\,-\,CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\big)/z} \,di,
$$ {#eq-hi}

where $z$ is the thermal sensitivity and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$ is the static temperature at which 50% of the population reaches the assayed endpoint after one hour of exposure. When $HI(t) = 100\%$, the cumulative dose equals one $t_{50}$ at the 1-hour reference, predicting 50% mortality (or 50% loss of the assayed sublethal endpoint, e.g.\ fertility; @orsted_suzukii_2024). Injury accumulates only when $T(i)$ exceeds a critical temperature $T_c$ below which damage is repaired as fast as it accrues [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022]. Field temperature records are typically available at hourly resolution. @eq-hi can then be evaluated by summing $100 \cdot 10^{(T_i - CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}})/z}$ across each hourly interval whose temperature $T_i$ exceeds $T_c$, following the implementations in @jorgensen_unifying_2021, @orsted_suzukii_2024, and @li2023. To propagate uncertainty, $z$ and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$, calculated from our Bayesian dose-response model can be used to calculate $HI(t)$ through time by substituting samples from the posterior distribution for each parameter into @eq-hi. 

A more natural prediction to make may instead be the population-level survival probability over time [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. This has the advantage of allowing for a direct connection to population dynamical models which typically use survival, growth and fecundity as vital rates for population-level predictions [@noble2026]. To predict survival, we can calculate the *effective exposure time* at a chosen reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$. More effective time is accumulated at hot temperatures and less at cool ones, in proportion to the scaling factor $10^{(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$,

$$
\tau_{\text{eff}}(t) \;=\; \int_0^t 10^{\,(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,di,
$$ {#eq-tau-eff}

We can then evaluate the 4PL surface once at the cumulative effective exposure to obtain the predicted population-level survival fraction at every point in the trajectory:

$$
S_{\text{pred}}(t) \;=\; S\!\big(T_{\text{ref}},\, \tau_{\text{eff}}(t)\big).
$$ {#eq-S-pred}

Here $S(T, \tau)$ is the survival surface fitted by the dose-response model, so $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ is the survival probability for an organism held at *constant* $T_{\text{ref}}$ after exposure duration $\tau_{\text{eff}}(t)$. 

# Simulations Evaluating statistical properties of the joint and two-stage modelling approaches

We assessed the performance of the 4PL and two-stage approaches by estimating bias and 95% interval coverage for *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ across simulation replicates. Bias was calculated as the estimated value minus the true value, whereas coverage was defined as the proportion of intervals containing the true parameter value. The 4PL approach estimated parameter uncertainty directly from the fitted model. In contrast, the two-stage approach required uncertainty to be propagated using the Delta method. For the two-stage estimates, we constructed intervals using both normal and *t*-distribution quantiles to evaluate the sensitivity of coverage to different assumptions about sampling uncertainty.

We tested the performance of the two-stage and 4PL approaches across scenarios designed to separate different sources of estimator bias and uncertainty. First, we simulated a strict-equivalence baseline in which survival spanned the full 0–1 range, there was no overdispersion, and only the dose-response midpoint varied with temperature. This scenario tests whether the two approaches recover the same quantities when the assumptions of the classical two-stage model are met. Second, we introduced beta-binomial overdispersion while keeping the same underlying dose-response shape, allowing us to isolate the cost of likelihood misspecification when binomial models are applied to overdispersed count data. Third, we simulated departures from the classical dose-response assumptions by allowing the upper asymptote to decline with temperature, both asymptotes to compress with temperature, or the curve steepness to change with temperature. These scenarios reflect empirical cases in which survival or sublethal responses do not span the full response range, or where the shape of the dose-response curve changes across assay temperatures. Finally, we ran sensitivity analyses that varied the strength of upper-asymptote drift, the overall level of the upper asymptote, and different experimental designs. 

Experimental designs were chosen to span the scale of published TDT/TLS assays, which often estimate response curves from several static temperatures and exposure durations with modest numbers of individuals, samples, or replicate vials per treatment [@truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @faber_tdt_plants_2024]. In the simulations, designs included one to five replicate cups per temperature-by-time treatment, with 10 to 20 organisms per cup sampled from a uniform distribution. For each scenario-by-design combination, we simulated 1,000 datasets and fit both the two-stage and 4PL approaches. Overall, we ran 25,000 simulations and were limited to 1000 simulations per run. We report Monte Carlo standard errors (MCSE) for the bias and coverage summaries to better understand across simulation uncertainty [@williams2024; @direnzo2023; @koehler2009].

#### *Simulation results*

When the data-generating process matched the classical assumptions, with survival spanning the full 0-1 range and temperature affecting only the dose-response midpoint, the two-stage approach performed similarly to the joint 4PL model, producing little bias and near-nominal coverage when confidence intervals were constructed with t-distribution quantiles. This distinction was important: using normal (z) quantiles instead produced anti-conservative intervals with coverage well below the nominal 95% level (@fig-sims & Supplement 3.1 & 3.2).

In contrast, the two-stage approach resulted in biased *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, often with poor coverage, when temperature impacted upper thresholds (up) and rate of decline (k) (@fig-sims). These biases were consistent with larger sample sizes (@fig-sims), but were exacerbated with fewer temperature time combinatons and low levels of replication (See Supplement 3.6). Bias for both *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from the two-stage approaches were particularly severe as the upper survival threshold decreased (@fig-sims2). 

Overall, these results confirm that any model misspecfication requires the 4PL to properly recover true *z* and $CT_{max}$, with correct uncertainty. The 4PL model can be fit with the `bayesTLS` package we provide (see Supplement 1.1), defaulting to temperature dependence on all parameters of the 4PL. If no temperaure effect exists on a given parameter it will be estimated at zero and it will be equivalent to simplier models. These models also allow for more sophisicated modelling that the two-stage approaches cannot achieve easily. In our supplement, we also show how readers can build greater flexibility into their models if this is required. 

```{r}
#| label: fig-sims
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6.5
#| fig-cap: "Simulation performance when the data-generating process violates classical TDT shape assumptions. Row labelled **'u varies with T'**: upper asymptote $u$ declines 0.01 per °C from 0.92 at $T=34$ °C, with $\\text{low}=0.05$ and $k=8$ constant in $T$. Row labelled **'k varies with T'**: slope $k$ ranges from 6 at 30 °C to 10 at 38 °C, with $u=0.92$ and $\\text{low}=0.05$ constant. Both rows use 5 replicate cups per (T × duration) cell, $N_{sim}=1000$ simulated datasets per row, and beta-binomial overdispersion ($\\phi=5$). Panel (a): mean signed bias in $z$ (left column) and $CT_{max,1h}$ (right column) with 95% Monte Carlo SE bars. Panel (b): empirical 95% interval coverage with the same column layout. Dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal coverage. Absolute-scale methods (`joint_4pl_abs` and all two-stage variants) are evaluated against the analytical absolute-50% truth; `joint_4pl` is evaluated against the relative midpoint truth. Two-stage normal- and t-quantile variants share point estimates and differ only in Stage-2 delta-method interval construction."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + paired colour palette (matches notes writeup) --
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bin_t",
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

# Use n_reps = 5 cells of Scen 3 and Scen 5 for the manuscript figure.
.d <- dplyr::bind_rows(.load_screened("scen3"),
                       .load_screened("scen5")) |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("_n5$", scenario)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                labels = .method_labels),
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                labels = c("z (°C)",
                                           "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    scenario_label = dplyr::recode(
      sub("_n5$", "", scenario),
      scen3_heat_lowers_u = "u varies with T",
      scen5_sharpen       = "k varies with T"),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
    cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
  )

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate − truth)", y = NULL,
       title = "(a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")

.p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
  labs(x = "Empirical 95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
       title = "(b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

.p_bias / .p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-sims2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-cap: "Bias in $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ as the partial-mortality regime is swept along two orthogonal axes. Row labelled **'u drifts with T (varying $\\beta_u$)'**: upper asymptote at $T = \\bar T$ fixed at $u_0 = 0.92$, slope $\\beta_u$ varied across $\\{-0.005, -0.010, -0.015, -0.019\\}$ pp/°C — i.e., $u$ stays high at cool assays and declines progressively at hotter ones. Row labelled **'u is constant but reduced (varying $u_0$)'**: $\\beta_u = 0$ (no temperature drift); the baseline level itself is swept across $\\{0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99\\}$ — i.e., a constant fraction of the cohort is never susceptible, regardless of temperature. Both rows: $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, mid linear in $T$ with slope $-0.15$, beta-binomial likelihood ($\\phi=5$), $n_{reps}=5$, $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. Points are mean signed bias with 95% Monte Carlo SE bars; dashed lines mark zero bias. Left column: $z$; right column: $CT_{max,1h}$. Only the four distinct point-estimate methods are shown: the two-stage Normal-CI and t-CI variants share point estimates within each Stage-1 family, so bias is identical between them; coverage differences appear in the supplementary writeup. Methods are evaluated against the truth on their own threshold scale (see Methods). The two failure signatures differ: drifting $u$ tilts the Stage-2 slope (large $z$ bias, modest $CT_{max,1h}$ bias); constant low $u$ shifts the Stage-2 intercept (large $CT_{max,1h}$ bias, with $z$ bias of opposite sign growing as $u_0$ falls)."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + colour palette ---------------------------------
# Bias is identical for the Normal-CI and t-CI variants of each
# two-stage Stage-1 family (they share point estimates; only Stage-2 CI
# construction differs). For this bias-only figure we therefore plot
# just the four distinct point-estimate methods: two_stage_bin,
# two_stage_bb, joint_4pl (relative), joint_4pl_abs (absolute).
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bb",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin", "Two-stage BB",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)", "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f", "#a63603", "#006d2c", "#a1d99b"),
                         .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

.scen6 <- .load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 1000,
                sweep_label = "u drifts with T (varying β_u, pp / °C)")

.scen7 <- .load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100,
                sweep_label = "u is constant but reduced (varying u_0)")

.shape_fmt <- function(d) {
  d |>
    dplyr::filter(method %in% .method_levels) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                  labels = c("z (°C)",
                                             "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
      bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias
    )
}

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.build <- function(d, x_label, x_breaks) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = sweep_x, y = mean_bias, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = bias_lo, ymax = bias_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "Mean bias (estimate − truth)") +
    .theme_proj
}

.p6 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen6),
              x_label  = expression(beta[u]~"(pp / °C, drift of "*italic(u)*" with T)"),
              x_breaks = c(-0.019, -0.015, -0.010, -0.005)) +
  ggtitle("(a) u drifts with T (varying β_u)")

.p7 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen7),
              x_label  = expression(u[0]~"(constant upper asymptote, all T)"),
              x_breaks = c(0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99)) +
  ggtitle("(b) u is constant but reduced (varying u_0)")

.p6 / .p7 + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

# Application of Bayesian dose-response models to case studies

We applied the Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model to four case studies spanning lethal and sublethal thermal tolerance data. Across these examples, we show how the model estimates classical TLS quantities, including $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$, while estimating and propagating uncertainty to heat-injury and survival predictions. We implemented this workflow in the R package `bayesTLS`; the [online supplement](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/) provides a package introduction detailing how it can be downloaded and a detailed tutorial using simulated data and the empirical case studies.

The four example case studies we use to validate our modelling approach are: 1) brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), with lethal mortality and sublethal knockdown-time data collected across temperature-duration treatments; 2) zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) lethal data across early life stages; 3) snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) leaf photosystem II performance, measured through chlorophyll-fluorescence-based photosynthetic efficiency; and 4) vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) mortality and sublethal-response data from @orsted_suzukii_2024. In each case study, we estimate $z$, $CT_{max}$, heat injury, and survival – all with associated uncertainty. In the zebrafish and vinegar fly examples, we also show how the same model estimates and compares group-specific TLS parameters across life stages and sexes. There is also interest in estimating $T_{crit}$ in the literature [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019]. $T_{crit}$ can be approximated from lethal data by using the TDT models to estimate the tranistion point where heat injury begins to accumulate. While there are different methods for estimating $T_{crit}$, our method makes use of existing TDT experimental data. 

For the empirical analyses, we fit @eq-4pl in `brms` through `bayesTLS`, adding fixed effects and random intercepts to the 4PL parameter predictors where needed. For example, the midpoint can be written as

$$
\text{mid}_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1(T_{ij} - \bar T) + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\gamma} + b_j,
$$ {#eq-linpred}

where $\mathbf{x}_{ij}$ contains fixed covariates and $b_j$ is a group-level random intercept. Count responses were fit with a beta-binomial likelihood with identity link (brown shrimp, zebrafish, and vinegar fly), while the continuous snow gum PSII proportions were fit with a Beta likelihood with identity link. Random intercepts on $\text{mid}$ accounted for date and tank effects in brown shrimp, experimental date in zebrafish, and day and glasshouse-room effects in snow gum. For group comparisons, `life_stage` in zebrafish and `sex` in vinegar fly were included on $\text{mid}$, with temperature interactions used to estimate group-specific $z$ and $CT_{max}$. To keep the 4PL identifiable, `bayesTLS` constrains $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ to disjoint intervals and estimates $k$ on a positive scale.

The main case-study models used four MCMC chains with 8000 iterations per chain, including 4000 warmup iterations, and `seed = 123`. The custom joint vinegar-fly sex model used the same Bayesian 4PL structure but a shorter run of 3000 iterations with 1000 warmup iterations. Full formulas, priors, sampler controls, and diagnostics are reported in the online supplement.

## Thermal sensitivities across species, sexes and life stages






## What the 4PL delivers that the two-step cannot

# Worked example: brown shrimp thermal tolerance

<!--
Multi-endpoint demonstration on Crangon crangon.
Source pipeline: R/bayesian_TDT_shrimp.qmd.
-->

## Data and experimental design

<!-- Placeholder: a flextable summarising n trials, n individuals, temperature
     range, and exposure range for each of the three endpoints will be added
     here once the case-study analyses are complete. The chunk is omitted
     while empty so it doesn't produce a dangling cross-reference. -->


## Lethal TDT — hierarchical 4PL on counts

```{r}
#| label: fig-survival-curves
#| fig-cap: "Posterior survival curves at each lethal-TDT assay temperature, with 95% credible bands and observed survival proportions."
#| eval: false
```

## Sublethal time-to-death

## Dynamic $CT_{max}$ across ramping rates

## Reconciling the three endpoints via the Jørgensen ramping equation

```{r}
#| label: fig-tdt-ctmax-reconciliation
#| fig-cap: "Forward (TDT → dynamic $CT_{max}$) and inverse ($CT_{max}$ → TDT) reconciliation under full posterior propagation."
#| eval: false
```

## Field-temperature heat-injury projections under warming scenarios

```{r}
#| label: fig-hi-warming
#| fig-cap: "Posterior cumulative heat-injury and survival under measured Baltic surface-temperature traces and +1, +2, +3 °C warming scenarios, with and without a Sharpe–Schoolfield repair kernel."
#| eval: false
```

# Discussion

## What changes for the field if the hierarchical 4PL becomes the default

## Limits of the present analysis

<!--
Honest discussion of:
- Repair kernel parameters fixed, not fit
- d$CT_{max}$ reconciliation uses nls() per draw rather than a joint Stan likelihood
- Arrhenius (1/T-Kelvin) sensitivity check belongs in the supplement
- Endpoint heterogeneity is not eliminated, only made visible
-->

## Recommendations for primary studies and meta-analyses

# Conclusions

# Acknowledgements

We would like to thank Johannes Overgaard, Joey Bernhard, Wilco Verbeck, Jennifer Sunday, Ignacio Peralta-Maraver, and Arnaud Sentis for helpful discussions through the development of this paper. 

# Author contributions {.unnumbered}

# Data and code availability {.unnumbered}

All data, analysis scripts, and the rendered manuscript and supplement are available at https://github.com/daniel1noble/bayesTLS. The detailed supplement and tutorial, along with details on downloading the `bayesTLS` R package, can also be found here: https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/. 

# Conflict of interest {.unnumbered}

The authors declare no competing interests.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
